expanded_description: Understanding how scale factors affect the surface areas and volumes of similar solids through the relationships k² and k³

core_concept.principle: When two solids are similar with scale factor k, their surface areas are in the ratio k² and their volumes are in the ratio k³
core_concept.explanation: Similar solids have the same shape but different sizes. If the ratio of corresponding linear dimensions (lengths, widths, heights, radii) is k, then the ratio of their surface areas is k² (the square of the scale factor), and the ratio of their volumes is k³ (the cube of the scale factor). This powerful relationship allows us to compare measurements without knowing exact dimensions.

image.alt: Two similar rectangular prisms showing scale factor k between corresponding dimensions
image.caption: Figure 1: Similar solids with scale factor k - linear dimensions ratio k, surface areas ratio k², volumes ratio k³

sections.section_title: Understanding Similar Solids and Scale Factors
sections.introduction: Two solids are similar if they have the same shape and their corresponding linear dimensions are proportional. The scale factor is the ratio of any two corresponding lengths.

key_concept.definition: Two solids are similar if and only if their corresponding angles are congruent and their corresponding linear dimensions are proportional
key_concept.context: The scale factor k is the ratio of corresponding linear measurements: $k = \frac{\text{length in solid A}}{\text{corresponding length in solid B}}$

image.alt: Two similar cylinders showing proportional radii and heights
image.caption: Figure 2: Similar cylinders where $\frac{r_1}{r_2} = \frac{h_1}{h_2} = k$

principles.statement: For two solids to be similar, ALL corresponding linear dimensions must have the same ratio k
principles.example: Two rectangular prisms with dimensions 3×4×5 and 6×8×10 are similar because $\frac{6}{3} = \frac{8}{4} = \frac{10}{5} = 2$
principles.analogy: Similar solids are like photographs of the same object at different sizes - everything is proportionally larger or smaller

worked_examples.title: Determining if Solids are Similar
worked_examples.problem: Determine if two rectangular prisms are similar. Prism A has dimensions 4 cm × 6 cm × 8 cm. Prism B has dimensions 10 cm × 15 cm × 20 cm. If similar, find the scale factor.

step_by_step.step: Set up ratios of corresponding dimensions
step_by_step.explanation: Compare length to length, width to width, height to height
Reduce each fraction to simplest form

step_by_step.step: Check if all ratios are equal
step_by_step.explanation: All three ratios equal 2.5, so the prisms are similar

worked_examples.final_answer: The prisms are similar with scale factor k = 2.5 (or $\frac{5}{2}$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Definition of similar solids - all corresponding linear dimensions must be proportional

worked_examples.title: Finding Missing Dimensions in Similar Solids
worked_examples.problem: Two similar cones have a scale factor of 3:2 (larger to smaller). If the smaller cone has radius 4 cm and height 9 cm, find the dimensions of the larger cone.

step_by_step.explanation: The ratio of larger to smaller is 3:2, so $k = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$

step_by_step.step: Find the radius of the larger cone
step_by_step.explanation: Multiply the smaller radius by the scale factor

step_by_step.step: Find the height of the larger cone
step_by_step.explanation: Multiply the smaller height by the scale factor

worked_examples.final_answer: The larger cone has radius 6 cm and height 13.5 cm
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using scale factor to find corresponding linear dimensions

image.caption: Figure 3: Similar cones with scale factor 3:2

sections.section_title: Ratio of Surface Areas - The k² Relationship
sections.introduction: When solids are similar with scale factor k, their surface areas are related by k². This is because surface area is a two-dimensional measurement.

key_concept.definition: If two similar solids have a scale factor of k, then the ratio of their surface areas is k²
key_concept.context: Since surface area involves squaring linear dimensions (like length × width), the scale factor is squared

image.alt: Diagram showing surface area ratio equals scale factor squared
image.caption: Figure 4: If scale factor is k, then $\frac{SA_1}{SA_2} = k^2$

principles.statement: For similar solids with scale factor k: $\frac{\text{Surface Area}_1}{\text{Surface Area}_2} = k^2$
principles.application: Use this to find surface area of one solid when you know the surface area of a similar solid and the scale factor
principles.example: If two similar spheres have scale factor 3, their surface areas are in ratio $3^2 = 9$. If the smaller has SA = 20 cm², the larger has SA = 180 cm²
principles.analogy: If you double the size of a gift box (k=2), you need 4 times (2²) as much wrapping paper

worked_examples.title: Finding Surface Area Ratio from Scale Factor
worked_examples.problem: Two similar pyramids have a scale factor of 4:3. What is the ratio of their surface areas?

step_by_step.explanation: The scale factor k = 4/3
The ratio of surface areas is k²

worked_examples.final_answer: The ratio of surface areas is 16:9

worked_examples.title: Finding Surface Area Using k²
worked_examples.problem: Two similar cylinders are shown. The scale factor from Cylinder A to Cylinder B is 2:5. If Cylinder A has surface area 48π cm², find the surface area of Cylinder B.

step_by_step.explanation: From A to B, the scale factor is $k = \frac{5}{2}$
Square the scale factor to get the SA ratio
$\frac{SA_B}{SA_A} = \frac{25}{4}$
Multiply both sides by 48π

worked_examples.final_answer: The surface area of Cylinder B is 300π cm² (approximately 942.48 cm²)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using k² relationship to find unknown surface area

image.alt: Two similar cylinders with labeled surface areas
image.caption: Figure 5: Similar cylinders demonstrating surface area ratio

worked_examples.title: Finding Scale Factor from Surface Areas
worked_examples.problem: Two similar spheres have surface areas of 36π in² and 144π in². Find the scale factor from the smaller to the larger sphere.

step_by_step.step: Set up the surface area ratio
step_by_step.explanation: Divide the larger SA by the smaller SA
Since $\frac{SA_2}{SA_1} = k^2$, we have $k^2 = 4$
Solve for k by taking the square root of both sides

worked_examples.concept_applied: Finding scale factor by taking square root of surface area ratio

image.alt: Two spheres with given surface areas
image.caption: Figure 6: Finding scale factor from known surface areas

sections.section_title: Ratio of Volumes - The k³ Relationship
sections.introduction: Volume is a three-dimensional measurement, so when solids are similar with scale factor k, their volumes are related by k³.

key_concept.definition: If two similar solids have a scale factor of k, then the ratio of their volumes is k³
key_concept.context: Since volume involves cubing linear dimensions (like length × width × height), the scale factor is cubed

image.alt: Diagram showing volume ratio equals scale factor cubed
image.caption: Figure 7: If scale factor is k, then $\frac{V_1}{V_2} = k^3$

principles.statement: For similar solids with scale factor k: $\frac{\text{Volume}_1}{\text{Volume}_2} = k^3$
principles.application: Use this to find volume of one solid when you know the volume of a similar solid and the scale factor
principles.example: If two similar cubes have scale factor 2, their volumes are in ratio $2^3 = 8$. If the smaller has V = 10 cm³, the larger has V = 80 cm³
principles.analogy: If you triple the size of a fish tank (k=3), it holds 27 times (3³) as much water

worked_examples.title: Finding Volume Ratio from Scale Factor
worked_examples.problem: Two similar cones have a scale factor of 2:5. What is the ratio of their volumes?

step_by_step.explanation: The scale factor k = 2/5
The ratio of volumes is k³

worked_examples.title: Finding Volume Using k³
worked_examples.problem: Two similar prisms have a scale factor of 3:4 (smaller to larger). If the smaller prism has volume 135 m³, find the volume of the larger prism.

step_by_step.explanation: From smaller to larger, $k = \frac{4}{3}$
Cube the scale factor to get the volume ratio
$\frac{V_{\text{larger}}}{V_{\text{smaller}}} = \frac{64}{27}$
Multiply both sides by 135

worked_examples.final_answer: The volume of the larger prism is 320 cubic meters
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using k³ relationship to find unknown volume

worked_examples.title: Finding Scale Factor from Volumes
worked_examples.problem: Two similar spheres have volumes of 288π cm³ and 108π cm³. Find the scale factor from the larger to the smaller sphere.

step_by_step.explanation: Divide the larger volume by the smaller volume
Since $\frac{V_1}{V_2} = k^3$, we have $k^3 = \frac{8}{3}$
Solve for k by taking the cube root of both sides

worked_examples.final_answer: The scale factor is $\frac{2}{\sqrt[3]{3}}$ or approximately 1.39:1
worked_examples.concept_applied: Finding scale factor by taking cube root of volume ratio

image.caption: Figure 8: Finding scale factor from known volumes

worked_examples.title: Complete Problem: From Linear to Area to Volume
worked_examples.problem: Two similar pyramids have corresponding heights of 6 cm and 15 cm. The smaller pyramid has surface area 72 cm² and volume 96 cm³. Find the surface area and volume of the larger pyramid.

step_by_step.explanation: Use the ratio of corresponding heights
Square the scale factor
Multiply smaller SA by k²
Cube the scale factor
Multiply smaller volume by k³

worked_examples.final_answer: The larger pyramid has surface area 450 cm² and volume 1500 cm³
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying both k² and k³ relationships in one problem

image.alt: Two similar pyramids with all measurements labeled
image.caption: Figure 9: Complete example showing linear, area, and volume relationships

sections.introduction: The relationships between scale factor, surface area, and volume have important real-world applications in design, manufacturing, and biology.

key_concept.definition: Understanding k² and k³ relationships helps solve practical problems involving scaling objects up or down
key_concept.context: These principles explain why large animals have different proportions than small ones, and why scaling recipes or models requires careful calculation

image.alt: Examples of scaling in real world - model car, architectural model, etc.
image.caption: Figure 10: Real-world applications of similar solids

worked_examples.title: Model Building Application
worked_examples.problem: An architect builds a scale model of a building where 1 inch represents 8 feet. If the model uses 240 square inches of material for the exterior walls, how much material will be needed for the actual building?

step_by_step.step: Convert to same units and find scale factor
step_by_step.explanation: 1 inch : 8 feet = 1 inch : 96 inches, so $k = 96$ (actual to model)
Square the scale factor
Multiply model SA by k²
Divide by 144 (since 1 ft² = 144 in²)

worked_examples.final_answer: The actual building will need 15,360 square feet of material for the exterior walls
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using k² for surface area in scale model applications

worked_examples.title: Manufacturing and Cost Analysis
worked_examples.problem: A company manufactures two similar spherical storage tanks. The smaller tank has a diameter of 2 meters and costs $500 to manufacture. The larger tank has a diameter of 5 meters. If manufacturing cost is proportional to surface area, estimate the cost of the larger tank.

step_by_step.explanation: Use the ratio of diameters
Square the scale factor
Since cost ∝ surface area, multiply the smaller cost by k²

worked_examples.final_answer: The larger tank will cost approximately $3,125 to manufacture
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying k² relationship to cost proportional to surface area

image.alt: Two similar spherical tanks with different sizes
image.caption: Figure 11: Similar spherical storage tanks

worked_examples.title: Biology: Surface Area to Volume Ratio
worked_examples.problem: Two similar cells are approximately spherical. Cell A has radius 10 μm and Cell B has radius 30 μm. Compare their surface area to volume ratios. Which cell can more efficiently exchange materials with its environment?

step_by_step.explanation: From Cell A to Cell B, $k = \frac{30}{10} = 3$

step_by_step.step: Calculate SA:V ratio for Cell A
step_by_step.explanation: For a sphere: $SA = 4\pi r^2$ and $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, so $\frac{SA}{V} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{r}$

step_by_step.step: Calculate SA:V ratio for Cell B
step_by_step.explanation: Use the same formula
Cell A has SA:V ratio of 0.3, Cell B has 0.1. Cell A's ratio is 3 times larger.

worked_examples.final_answer: Cell A has a surface area to volume ratio 3 times greater than Cell B. Cell A can more efficiently exchange materials with its environment because it has more surface area relative to its volume.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding why smaller similar objects have better SA:V ratios

image.alt: Two cells showing different SA:V ratios
image.caption: Figure 12: Smaller cells have better surface area to volume ratios for material exchange

process_flow.description: Find the ratio of any two corresponding linear dimensions
process_flow.result: Scale factor k (a pure number, no units)

process_flow.process: Determine what you need to find
process_flow.description: Identify whether you need linear dimension, surface area, or volume
process_flow.result: Clear understanding of which relationship to use

process_flow.process: Apply the appropriate power of k
process_flow.description: Use k for linear, k² for area, k³ for volume
process_flow.result: Correct ratio for the measurement type

process_flow.description: Calculate the answer and verify it makes sense
process_flow.organization: If k > 1, the second solid is larger; if k < 1, the second solid is smaller

summary: When two solids are similar, all corresponding linear dimensions have the same ratio k, called the scale factor. This scale factor determines how surface areas and volumes compare: the ratio of surface areas is k² (the scale factor squared), and the ratio of volumes is k³ (the scale factor cubed). These relationships work because surface area is two-dimensional (involving products of two lengths) while volume is three-dimensional (involving products of three lengths). To solve problems: (1) find the scale factor from linear dimensions, (2) square it for surface area comparisons or cube it for volume comparisons, (3) set up proportions to find unknown values. Conversely, if given surface area ratio, take the square root to find k; if given volume ratio, take the cube root. These principles are essential in fields ranging from architecture and manufacturing to biology and design.
